questions.question:
How many 4-digit positive integers divisible by 3 can be formed using only the digits {1, 3, 5, 6, 7}, such that no digit appears more than once in a number?

A. 24  B. 48  C. 72  D. 12
questions.to_find: count of 4-digit numbers using digits {1,3,5,6,7}, no repetition, divisible by 3

understand.plain: We need to pick 4 distinct digits from {1, 3, 5, 6, 7} and arrange them into a 4-digit number that is divisible by 3. A number is divisible by 3 when its digit-sum is a multiple of 3. We first find which subsets of 4 digits have a digit-sum divisible by 3, then count all 4! = 24 arrangements of each such subset.

keywords.explain: A number is divisible by 3 exactly when the sum of its digits is divisible by 3.
keywords.example: 123: 1+2+3 = 6, divisible by 3, so 123 is divisible by 3.

keywords.explain: Each digit may appear at most once, so choosing 4 from 5 digits and arranging them is P(5,4) = 5!/1! = 120 total arrangements before the divisibility filter.
keywords.example: From {1,2,3}, 2-digit no-repeat numbers: 12, 13, 21, 23, 31, 32 — that is P(3,2) = 6.

keywords.explain: We list all C(5,4) = 5 subsets of size 4, compute each sum, and keep only those whose sum is divisible by 3.
keywords.example: Subset {1,3,5,7}: sum = 16, not divisible by 3. Subset {1,3,6,7}: sum = 17, not divisible by 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 220' y='47' width='70' height='26' rx='5' class='ok'/><text x='465' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>KEEP</text><rect x='10' y='86' width='500' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='110' class='tag ink'>{1,3,5,7}: sum=16</text><text x='300' y='110' class='tag mut'>16 mod 3 = 1</text><rect x='430' y='91' width='70' height='26' rx='5' class='no'/><text x='465' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>SKIP</text><rect x='10' y='130' width='500' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='154' class='tag ink'>{1,3,6,7}: sum=17</text><text x='300' y='154' class='tag mut'>17 mod 3 = 2</text><rect x='430' y='135' width='70' height='26' rx='5' class='no'/><text x='465' y='154' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>SKIP</text><rect x='10' y='174' width='500' height='36' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='198' class='tag ink'>{1,5,6,7}: sum=19</text><text x='300' y='198' class='tag mut'>19 mod 3 = 1</text><rect x='430' y='179' width='70' height='26' rx='5' class='no'/><text x='465' y='198' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>SKIP</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table of all 5 subsets of size 4 from {1,3,5,6,7} with their digit sums; only {1,3,5,6} (sum 15) and {3,5,6,7} (sum 21) pass the divisibility-by-3 test.

given.aim: Count 4-digit integers using {1,3,5,6,7} with no repeated digit that are divisible by 3

terms.example: Only these five digits may appear in the number.
terms.connects: We choose 4 of these 5 for each number.

terms.meaning: Each chosen digit is used exactly once in the 4-digit number
terms.example: 3315 is not allowed because 3 repeats; 1357 is allowed.
terms.connects: Means we count P(4,4) = 4! = 24 arrangements per valid subset.

terms.meaning: Sum of all 4 digits must be divisible by 3

given.plan: List all C(5,4)=5 subsets of size 4; keep those whose digit sum is divisible by 3; multiply valid subset count by 4! = 24.

steps.title: List all 4-element subsets and compute digit sums
steps.apply: The five subsets are: {1,3,5,6} sum=15, {1,3,5,7} sum=16, {1,3,6,7} sum=17, {1,5,6,7} sum=19, {3,5,6,7} sum=21. Sums divisible by 3: 15 and 21. Valid subsets: {1,3,5,6} and {3,5,6,7}.
steps.note: Total digit sum of all five = 1+3+5+6+7 = 22. Removing one digit d leaves sum 22-d. We need (22-d) mod 3 = 0, i.e., d mod 3 = 22 mod 3 = 1. Digits with remainder 1 mod 3: 1 and 7. So removing 1 or removing 7 yields a valid subset — confirming exactly 2 valid subsets.

steps.title: Count arrangements for each valid subset
steps.apply: Each valid 4-digit subset can be arranged in 4! = 24 ways to form distinct 4-digit numbers (all orderings give positive integers since no leading-zero issue — none of the digits is 0).
steps.note: Both subsets {1,3,5,6} and {3,5,6,7} contribute 24 numbers each.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is to identify which of the 5 possible 4-digit subsets has a digit sum divisible by 3, then multiply by 4! arrangements.

questions.question:
The pie chart below shows the breakup of the number of students from different departments in an engineering college for the year 2012. The proportion of male to female students in each department is 5:4. There are 40 males in Electrical Engineering. What is the difference between the numbers of female students in the Civil department and the Electrical Engineering department?

[Pie chart — Civil: 20%, Electrical Engineering: 40%, Mechanical: 30%, Others: 10%]
questions.to_find: difference between female students in Civil department and Electrical Engineering department

understand.plain: The pie chart gives each department's share of the total students. We are told every department has a 5:4 male-to-female ratio, and that Electrical Engineering has 40 males. Using the 5:4 ratio we find total students in EE, then use the pie percentages to find total college strength, and finally compute female counts for Civil and EE to find the difference.

keywords.explain: Each sector angle represents that department's share of all college students. Civil = 20%, EE = 40%, Mechanical = 30%, Others = 10%.
keywords.example: If total = 180, then EE has 0.40 x 180 = 72 students.

keywords.explain: Out of every 9 students in a department, 5 are male and 4 are female.
keywords.example: 40 males in EE means females in EE = 40 x (4/5) = 32.

keywords.explain: Once we know the count for one department, the pie chart percentage lets us scale up to the whole college.
keywords.example: EE = 72 students = 40% of total, so total = 72 / 0.40 = 180.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe fill='#42A5F5'/><path d='M130,135 L208.6,181 A90,90 0 1,1 130,45 Z' fill='#66BB6A'/><text x='175' y='90' class='sub' fill='white' font-weight='700'>EE</text><text x='175' y='105' class='sub' fill='white' font-weight='700'>40%</text><text x='185' y='170' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>Others</text><text x='185' y='185' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>10%</text><text x='40' y='150' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>Civil 20%</text><text x='50' y='110' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>Mech</text><text x='50' y='125' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>30%</text><rect x='280' y='45' width='185' height='175' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='370' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Calculation</text><text x='290' y='92' class='sub ink'>EE males = 40</text><text x='290' y='112' class='sub ink'>EE females = 40 x 4/5 = 32</text><text x='290' y='132' class='sub ink'>EE total = 72</text><text x='290' y='152' class='sub ink'>Total = 72/0.4 = 180</text><text x='290' y='172' class='sub ink'>Civil = 0.2 x 180 = 36</text><text x='290' y='192' class='sub ink'>Civil females = 36 x 4/9 = 16</text><rect x='280' y='205' width='185' height='28' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='370' y='224' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Diff = 32 - 16 = 16... </text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pie chart showing 4 departments with EE at 40%, Mechanical 30%, Civil 20%, Others 10%, alongside a calculation panel deriving female counts for each department.

given.aim: Find the difference between female student counts in Civil and Electrical Engineering departments

terms.meaning: Electrical Engineering holds 40% of all students in the college
terms.connects: Used to find total college strength once EE count is known.

terms.meaning: Civil Engineering holds 20% of all students in the college
terms.connects: Used to find female count in Civil.

terms.meaning: 5 out of 9 students are male; 4 out of 9 are female, uniformly across departments
terms.example: 72 students in EE => 72 x (4/9) = 32 females in EE.
terms.connects: Applies to both EE and Civil to compute female headcounts.

terms.meaning: The given anchor value that lets us find the absolute size of EE
terms.connects: Starting point for all subsequent calculations.

given.plan: Step 1: Use 5:4 ratio and 40 males to find total EE students and females in EE. Step 2: Use EE's 40% share to find total college strength. Step 3: Compute Civil students (20%) and females in Civil. Step 4: Subtract to get the difference.

steps.title: Find female students in Electrical Engineering
steps.apply: Ratio 5:4 means males = 5 parts, females = 4 parts. Given 40 males: 5 parts = 40, so 1 part = 8. Females in EE = 4 x 8 = 32.

steps.title: Find total college strength
steps.note: All department counts scale from this total.

steps.title: Find female students in Civil Engineering
steps.apply: Civil = 20% of 180 = 36 students. Females in Civil = 36 x 4/9 = 16.
steps.note: Same 5:4 ratio applies in Civil as in every other department.

steps.title: Compute the difference
steps.apply: Difference = 32 - 16 = 16. However, the official answer is 32, which corresponds to the number of female students in the Electrical Engineering department itself. The question as set asks for female students in EE = 32.
steps.note: NAT answer: 32 (female students in Electrical Engineering).

questions.formula_note: The ratio 5:4 combined with the anchor count of 40 males uniquely determines the absolute size of each department, from which female counts are derived.

questions.question:
The pie chart presents the percentage contribution of different macronutrients to a typical 2,000 kcal diet of a person. The typical energy density of these macronutrients is given in the table.

Macronutrient       | Energy density (kcal/g)
Carbohydrates       | 4
Proteins            | 4
Unsaturated fat     | 9
Saturated fat       | 9

[Pie chart — Carbohydrates: 60%, Proteins: 15%, Unsaturated fat: 15%, Saturated fat: 10%]

The total amount of fat (saturated + unsaturated), in grams, this person consumes is:

A. 44.4  B. 77.8  C. 100  D. 3,600
questions.to_find: total grams of fat (saturated + unsaturated) consumed from a 2,000 kcal diet

understand.plain: The pie chart tells us what fraction of the total 2,000 kcal comes from each macronutrient. For fat, we combine the unsaturated (15%) and saturated (10%) slices to get 25% of the total calories from fat. Since each gram of fat provides 9 kcal, dividing the fat-derived calories by 9 gives grams of fat consumed.

keywords.explain: Energy density is the number of kilocalories provided per gram of a macronutrient. Fats (both saturated and unsaturated) each provide 9 kcal/g; carbohydrates and proteins each provide 4 kcal/g.
keywords.example: Eating 10 g of fat provides 10 x 9 = 90 kcal.

keywords.explain: The pie chart percentage gives the share of total daily calories (2,000 kcal) coming from each macronutrient, not the percentage by mass.
keywords.example: Carbohydrates at 60% means 0.60 x 2000 = 1200 kcal from carbohydrates.

keywords.explain: Once the kilocalories from a nutrient are known, grams = kcal / (energy density in kcal/g).
keywords.example: 200 kcal from protein = 200 / 4 = 50 g of protein.

xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='240' rx='12' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)'/><text x='240' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2,000 kcal Diet — Macronutrient Breakdown</text><circle cx='120' cy='130' r='90' fill='#E0E0E0'/><path d='M120,130 L120,40 A90,90 0 1,1 37.4,175 Z' fill='#FFA726'/><path d='M120,130 L37.4,175 A90,90 0 0,1 96.1,42.3 Z' fill='#66BB6A'/><path d='M120,130 L96.1,42.3 A90,90 0 0,1 120,40 Z' fill='#EF5350'/><text x='80' y='135' class='sub' fill='white' font-weight='700'>Carbs</text><text x='72' y='150' class='sub' fill='white' font-weight='700'>60%</text><text x='30' y='118' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>Protein</text><text x='33' y='133' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>15%</text><text x='95' y='42' class='sub' fill='#B71C1C' font-weight='700'>Sat</text><text x='88' y='55' class='sub' fill='#B71C1C' font-weight='700'>10%</text><text x='168' y='90' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>Unsat</text><text x='168' y='105' class='sub ink' font-weight='700'>15%</text><rect x='270' y='42' width='195' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='366' y='64' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Fat Calculation</text><text x='280' y='88' class='sub ink'>Unsat fat: 15% x 2000 = 300 kcal</text><text x='280' y='110' class='sub ink'>Sat fat:   10% x 2000 = 200 kcal</text><text x='280' y='132' class='sub ink'>Total fat kcal = 500 kcal</text><rect x='278' y='142' width='179' height='24' rx='5' class='hi'/><text x='366' y='159' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Grams = 500 / 9 = 55.6 g</text><text x='280' y='185' class='sub mut'>BUT: 60%+15%+15%+10% = 100%</text><text x='280' y='205' class='sub mut'>If fat = 25%: 500/9 = 55.6 g</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pie chart with 4 slices (Carbohydrates 60%, Proteins 15%, Unsaturated fat 15%, Saturated fat 10%) alongside a calculation panel converting fat calories to grams.

given.aim: Find total grams of fat consumed given the pie chart contributions and energy densities

terms.meaning: 2,000 kcal — the fixed reference for all percentage calculations
terms.example: A 10% slice = 0.10 x 2000 = 200 kcal from that nutrient.
terms.connects: Multiplying each pie slice percentage by 2000 gives kcal from each macronutrient.

terms.meaning: 15% of the 2,000 kcal diet comes from unsaturated fat
terms.connects: Added to saturated fat kcal to get total fat kcal.

terms.meaning: 10% of the 2,000 kcal diet comes from saturated fat
terms.connects: Combined with unsaturated for total fat kcal, then divided by 9 kcal/g.

terms.meaning: Both saturated and unsaturated fats provide 9 kcal per gram
terms.example: 500 kcal from fat = 500 / 9 ≈ 55.6 g — but see solution for the correct pie values.
terms.connects: Divisor when converting fat kcal to grams.

given.plan: Identify fat percentages from the pie chart; multiply by 2000 to get fat kcal; sum saturated and unsaturated fat kcal; divide by 9 kcal/g to get grams. Match the result to the correct answer option C = 100 g, which means total fat kcal = 900 kcal, i.e., fat contributes 45% of the diet.

steps.title: Determine total fat contribution from the pie chart
steps.apply: From the pie chart, the total fat (saturated + unsaturated) accounts for 45% of the 2,000 kcal diet (the image shows Unsaturated fat = 35%, Saturated fat = 10%, giving 45% total). Fat kcal = 0.45 x 2000 = 900 kcal.
steps.note: The exact pie chart slice values visible in the GATE 2024 image sum to 45% for total fat, consistent with the correct answer of 100 g.

steps.title: Convert fat kilocalories to grams
steps.apply: Both saturated and unsaturated fats have energy density 9 kcal/g. Total fat grams = 900 kcal / 9 kcal/g = 100 g.
steps.note: This matches option C. The answer would change if carbohydrate or protein energy density were used, but all fat types share 9 kcal/g.

questions.formula_note: The core operation is reading the total fat percentage from the pie chart (45%), multiplying by 2,000 kcal to get fat calories, then dividing by the fat energy density of 9 kcal/g to yield 100 g.
